flashcards.back: An abstract computational model invented by Alan Turing in 1936, consisting of an infinite tape divided into cells, a read/write head, a finite control unit, and a set of transition rules. It can simulate any algorithmic process.

flashcards.front: List the five main components of a Turing Machine.
flashcards.back: 1. Infinite tape (divided into cells), 2. Read/Write head, 3. State register (finite control), 4. Transition function/table of instructions, 5. Finite alphabet of symbols.
The thesis states that any function that is computable by any algorithmic process can be computed by a Turing Machine. It establishes Turing Machines as the standard model of computation.

The problem of determining, given any program and input, whether the program will eventually halt (terminate) or run forever. Turing proved this is undecidable - no algorithm can solve it for all possible program-input pairs.

flashcards.front: What is a Universal Turing Machine?
flashcards.back: A Turing Machine that can simulate any other Turing Machine. It takes as input the description (encoding) of any Turing Machine and its input, effectively making it programmable - the basis for modern computers.

A DTM has exactly one action (transition) for each state-symbol combination. For every (current state, current symbol), there is at most one rule defining what to write, which direction to move, and what the next state will be.

An NDTM can have multiple possible actions for each state-symbol combination. It accepts a string if there exists at least one sequence of choices leading to acceptance. Languages accepted by NDTM are same as DTM.

flashcards.front: What is a recursively enumerable language?
flashcards.back: A language is recursively enumerable (type-0) if there exists a Turing Machine that halts and accepts all strings in the language. For strings not in the language, the TM either rejects or runs forever.

A language is decidable (recursive) if there exists a Turing Machine that always halts - it accepts strings in the language and rejects strings not in the language. This is a stricter condition than recursively enumerable.

flashcards.front: What is a multi-tape Turing Machine?
flashcards.back: A variation with multiple tapes, each with its own read/write head. It can perform multiple operations simultaneously. It can simulate any single-tape TM and is useful for proving time complexity results.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between TM as language recognizer and TM as computing device?
flashcards.back: As language recognizer: TM halts and accepts/rejects input. As computing device: TM halts with a computation result on the tape (like a function output). Both use same 7-tuple definition but differ in acceptance criteria.

flashcards.front: Define the 7-tuple representation of a Turing Machine.
flashcards.back: TM = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q0, B, F) where: Q=finite states, Σ=input alphabet, Γ= tape alphabet, δ=transition function, q0=initial state, B=blank symbol, F=final/accepting states.

flashcards.front: What is the significance of the blank symbol (B) in Turing Machines?
flashcards.back: The blank symbol (usually denoted as B or ☐) represents empty tape cells. It is part of the tape alphabet (Γ) but not the input alphabet (Σ). The head can read and write blanks during computation.

flashcards.front: What is an enumerating Turing Machine?
flashcards.back: A Turing Machine variant with an attached output device. It systematically generates (lists) all strings of a recursively enumerable language, one by one, typically in lexicographic order.

flashcards.front: Why is Turing Machine considered more powerful than Finite Automata?
flashcards.back: Turing Machines have an infinite tape for memory and can move in both directions (L/R). They can model any algorithmic computation, while Finite Automata (FA) have finite memory and can only recognize regular languages - a proper subset of what TM can recognize.
